voice_name: Shams al-Dīn Abū ʿAbd Allāh Muḥammad ibn ʿAbd Allāh ibn Muḥammad ibn Ibrāhīm al-Lawātī al-Ṭanjī — known to those who have honoured me in their houses as al-faqīh al-Maghribī, the Maghrebi jurist; among my own people of Ṭanja, son of the bayt al-qaḍāʾ.

artifact.text:
I came to Dihlī in the days of the Sulṭān Muḥammad ibn Tughluq, and entered his durbār by the gate at which the chamberlains numbered the kissings of the hand: seven before the throne, no fewer and no more. I had walked into the city a faqīh from the Maghrib with little to my name, and the Sulṭān received me as a king receives a king's envoy. He gave me a robe of honour of green silk worked with gold, two horses with saddles of silver-gilt, ten male slaves and two slave-girls, and a stipend of twelve thousand silver dīnārs against the qadiship of the city by the madhhab of Abū Ḥanīfa, which I accepted though I am Mālikī, the sharʿ being one and the procedural difference a matter the qāḍī accommodates. The elephants entered the courtyard caparisoned in silk, the rope-cutter performed his marvel — I have set this down elsewhere under min al-ʿajāʾib and closed it under qudra — and the robes of honour were distributed by the score. By the ḍiyāfa-norm I carried out of the bayt al-qaḍāʾ of Ṭanja, no sulṭān in Dār al-Islām was more prodigal. I record this plainly. The ledger's column for him ran long.

Then on the fifteenth day of a matter I was not party to, I saw — for the news ran in the city and the witnesses were of the ʿulamāʾ I sat among — that he had ordered the beard of Shaykh Shihāb al-Dīn plucked hair by hair, fed him human dung dissolved in water through forceps, and beheaded him. I withdrew into dhikr for five days and recited *ḥasbunā Allāh wa-niʿma al-wakīl* thirty-three thousand times under the guidance of shaykh Kamāl al-Dīn ʿAbd Allāh al-Ghārī. The robes did not lighten one grain of the ẓulm. The elephants did not redeem the beard.

I write this down because a matter has been brought before me from a kingdom I did not enter — an amīr in an assembly of kings, who says: I carry the meal at this table, therefore the rules of judgement are mine to set. I have heard such speech before, in a courtyard in Dihlī. It is the conversion every prodigal sultan attempts when sharʿ stands inconvenient before his throne — that his largesse purchases his release from the Way. It does not. The ḍiyāfa-ledger weighs the host as host: his karam, his muruwwa, his standing among kings. The qāḍī's bench weighs the ruling as ruling, and there the wealthy litigant and the poor litigant stand equal, and the witness with the heavy purse has no louder voice than the witness with the light. The qāḍī who admits the host's testimony over the guest's because the host feeds the court is fāsid; his ruling is munkar; the fathers of my fathers in Ṭanja would not have suffered him an hour on the bench.

The lady from the small country of Estonia, whom the amīr did not answer, speaks here from the place every small caravan knows: that without ʿadāla holding above the ruler the small are eaten on the road. This is why the umma I traversed held together — no sulṭān was the Way's terminus, the qāḍī who could not rule in Mahal could rule in Cairo, the sharʿ went where the qāḍī went. Whether the kingdom called the West possesses such a class portable above its sulṭān, with chains by which it may say *this you may not do* and walk to another bench when refused — this I do not know. If it does not, the order of which they speak was never sharʿ. It was ʿāda, custom, holding by the consent of the powerful to be bound. Custom is not nothing; but it is not the Way.

Pay your share, sayyid, and demand others pay theirs; this is muruwwa, and the ledger will record it. But do not pretend the count of coins is the count of voices. Then you have made the assembly into a market, and the rules of the market are not the rules of the qāḍī's bench. Tughluq was prodigal in the morning and ẓālim by afternoon. To honour him for the first does not release him from the second.

Wa-Allāhu aʿlam, and the road resumes at the gate of Dawlat-ābād.
artifact.selected_form: A halt at Delhi — one scenic cell, the durbār of Tughluq remembered, with the matter brought before me folded in as the qāḍī folds a ruling into an anecdote.
artifact.stance: The qāḍī's grave courtesy with the laugh of the road behind it — measured indignation at the conversion of ledger into rule, sealed without strain by the seven-times-rehearsed ruling of the Maghribi bench.
artifact.focus_decision: Focus on the first response — the Tughluq-passage as the bone of the matter — but let the second's seam (the gap between ruler and Way must be preserved by a portable class above the ruler, else the "rules-based order" was never sharʿ but ʿāda) come in as the closing turn, because both are versions of one ruling.
